prompt_text:
Daily Morning Pages: Write 3 pages longhand every
morning (set alarm 30 mins early)

Read the Basic Principles each morning and night. Be
aware of any skepticism or attitudinal shifts.

Take yourself on an Artist Date this week (something fun
and playful)

Describe or draw your childhood room - What's your
favorite thing about it? Could you add something like this to
your room now?
List 5 traits you like in yourself as a child

List 5 childhood accomplishments and 5 childhood favorite
foods - try eating one this week!

List three obvious and three subtle bad habits - examine
their payoffs and how to break them

List friends that nurture you and identify their beneficial
traits
Call a supportive friend who believes in your capabilities

Listen to your inner compass for an hour by doing an “artist
brain” activity. This is an activity that allows your brain to
relax and wander while you’re doing the activity.

List 5 people you admire openly and 5 you admire secretly -
what traits can you cultivate further in yourself?

List 5 people you wish you had met who are dead and 5
people who are dead who you'd like to hang out with for
eternity - what traits do you seek in friends?

Compare your two lists above and look at your true
admirations versus perceived obligations (things you think you should admire about these people) - follow your
authentic preference!

Record yourself reading the Basic Principles. Choose a favorite
essay from The Artist’s Way and record that too. Use for
meditation.

Handwrite your Artist's Prayer from Week Four. Keep it in your
wallet.

Get a creativity notebook and create 7 pages entitled: health,
possessions, leisure, relationships, creativity, career, and
spirituality. List 10 wishes for each category on the page.

Review the Honest Changes section (Week Four) and note how
you've changed since starting this program and recovery.
List 5 ways you will continue to change moving forward.

List 5 ways to nurture yourself in the next 6 months (courses,
supplies, artist's dates, vacations).

Plan one week of self-nurturing with one concrete loving action
each day.

Write and mail an encouraging letter to your inner artist.
Reexamine your concept of God/higher power. Does your belief
system limit or support your creative expansion? Are you
open-minded about altering your concept of God?

List ten examples of personal synchronicities that support the
possibility of a nurturing creative force.
The Artist’s Way

Goal Search Exercise:
1. Name your dream ("In a perfect world, | would secretly love to be a...")
2. Identify your "True North" - the concrete goal that signals
accomplishment
3. Write your 5-year vision for your dream
4. Create an action plan: This year, month, week, day, and right now
5. Choose a role model related to your dream

"New Childhood": Imagine perfect nurturing and write a page of this
fantasy childhood. What are ways to self-parent now?

Color Schemes: Choose a color and write a quick few sentences
describing yourself as that color (in the first person). What is your favorite color? What do you have that is that color?

List 5 "forbidden" activities and express them through art, writing, or
movement

Create your Style Search: List 20 activities you want to do. Answer these
questions for each item: Does it cost money or is it free? Expensive or
cheap? Alone or with somebody? Job related? Physical risk? Fast-paced
or slow? Mind, body, or spiritual? Ideal Day: Plan a perfect day in your life as it is now constituted, using the
information gleaned from above.

Ideal Ideal Day: Plan a perfect day in your life as you wish it were
constituted. There are no restriction.
Choose one festive aspect from your ideal day. Allow yourself to live it.

List Your Grievances: Write down 5 reasons why you struggle to
believe in a supportive God. Remember, honesty is key.

Create Your Dream Image File: List 5 desires you'd pursue if you had
faith or money. Collect images representing these dreams
throughout the week and put them into one file or page.

Revisit Imaginary Lives: Write down 5 alternative lives. Reflect on
any changes from previous weeks. Add visual representations to
your Dream Image File.

Twenty with Resources: Imagine being 20 with financial freedom.
List 5 adventures you'd undertake. Include these in your Dream
Image File.

Sixty-five with Means: Picture yourself at 65 with wealth. Note 5
delayed pleasures. Add these to your Dream Image File.

Self-Criticism Inventory: Document 10 ways you're unkind to
yourself. This exercise helps identify and release negative patterns.

Desire Catalog: List 10 items you wish to own. Gather images of
these desires and add them to your Dream Image File. Visualization
can help manifest goals.

Creative Block Illustration: Identify your favorite creative block (TV,
reading, etc.). Draw yourself engaging in this behavior.

Block Benefits: Explore what you gain from staying creatively
blocked. Reflect on this in Morning Pages.

Block Attribution: Identify who you blame for your creative blocks.
Process these thoughts in Morning Pages.

Artist Way Basic Principles
1. I am a channel for God’s creativity, and my work comes to good.
2. My dreams come from God and God has the power to accomplish them.
3. As I create and listen, I will be led.
4. Creativity is the Creator's will for me.
5. My creativity heals myself and others.
6. I am allowed to nurture my artist.
7. Through the use of a few simple tools, my creativity will flourish.
8. Through the use of my creativity, Iserve God.
9. My creativity always leads me to truth and love.
10. My creativity leads me to forgiveness and self-forgiveness."
11. There is a divine plan of goodness for me.
12. There is a divine plan of goodness for my work.
13. As I listen to the Creator within, I am led.
14. As I listen to my creativity I am led to my creator.
15. I am willing to create.
16. I am willing to learn to let myself create.
17. I am willing to let God create through me.
18. I am willing to be of service through my creativity.
19. I am willing to experience my creative energy.
20. I am willing to use my creative talents.

Time Analysis: List 5 major activities from this week and
analyze if these activities were things you should do or
want to do.

Draw a sphere and include names of supportive people.
List names of people that you need to be self-protective
from and place them outside of the circle

Create a list of 20 fun activities with the date you last did
that activity

Choose 2 activities from your list that you’ve avoided
and do them this week

Review Week 1 affirmations and write 3 chosen ones
daily in your morning pages.

Add 5 more Imaginary Lives to your list from Week 1
Make a Life Pie: Draw a circle and divide it evenly into 6
sections labeled spirituality, exercise, play, work, friends,
and romance/adventure. Fill in each section based on
how fulfilled you feel in that area.

10 Tiny Changes: List ten changes you’d like to make for
yourself, big or small

Choose one small change from your 10 Tiny Changes list
and make it your goal for your week

The Deadlies Exercise: Cut seven paper strips and write: alcohol, drugs, sex, work,
money, food, family/friends. Place in envelope. Draw one and write five negative
impacts it has had on your life. Repeat seven times (returning each slip after use).
There will be repeats and that’s the point.

Touchstones List: Create a list of things you love (your happiness touchstones). Post
it somewhere visible as a reminder. Consider drawing or acquiring one of these
items to enhance your space.
The Awful Truth Reflection. Answer these questions honestly:

1. What habit do you have that gets in the way of your creativity? Tell the truth.
2. What do you think might be a problem? It is.
3. What do you plan to do about the habit or problem?
4. What is your payoff in holding on to this block? If you can't figure out your payoff, ask a trusted friend. Tell the truth.
5. Which friends make you doubt yourself? (The self-doubt is yours already, but they trigger it.)
6. Which friends believe in you and your talent? (The talent is yours, but they make you feel it.)
7. What is the payoff in keeping your destructive friends?
8. If the answer is, "I like them," the next question is, "Why?"
9. Which destructive habits do your destructive friends share with your destructive self?
10. Which constructive habits do your constructive friends share with your
constructive self?

Setting a Bottom Line: Establish five firm boundaries based on your most painful
behaviors (based from answer above)

Cherishing Practice:
1. List five small victories
2. List three nurturing actions you took for your artist
3. List three potential comfort actions for your artist
4. Make three nice promises to yourself (and keep them)
5. Do one lovely thing for yourself each day this week

Review your Morning Pages using two colored markers - one for insights and one for action items - while maintaining a non-judgmental perspective and viewing them as an informative map of your journey.

Visualizing: Building on your work with goals and true north, immerse yourself fully in your accomplished vision. Take time to paint the scene in vivid detail - the sights,sounds, and feelings of your success.
1. Name your goal: I am ___
Write your ideal scene in present tense, capturing yourself at the peak of your powers. Make this vision concrete - read it aloud daily, post it where you'll see it.
Create a vision board this week, weaving together personal photos and inspiring images.

Priorities: Map out your creative journey in three horizons - yearly goals for your
grand vision, monthly goals for steady progress, and weekly goals for immediate
action.|
Creative U-Turns: We all have creative wounds. Name your abandoned projects: the minor ones, the major ones, the one that still aches.
Practice radical self-forgiveness for all past creative hesitations.
Create healing affirmations. Gently examine your creative graves. Which projects might be revived?
Choose one U-turn to reclaim. When resistance appears (Morning Pages feel pointless? Artist Date seems silly?), push through. Name your creative fears honestly.

4. Choose an artist totem (a cherished figurine or meaningful object), as a guardian
for your creative self. Give it a place of honor, and let it remind you to protect
your artistic spirit with the same care.

Environment: Describe your ideal environment in a
paragraph and find/create an image that represents it.
Include your favorite season and why.

Time Travel: Write about yourself at 80. What did you
accomplish? Write a letter from your 80-year-old self with
advice and encouragement.

Time Travel: Remember yourself at age 8. What did you
enjoy? Write a letter from your 8-year-old self.

Environment: Create a personal creative space at home.
Create a private area dedicated to creativity with
comfortable seating and inspiring elements.

Growth Check: Review your life drawing progress. Note
changes in activity and expression. List potential artist
treats like books, magazines, or tickets.
Write your own Artist's Prayer. Use it every day for a week.

Open your closet. Throw out—or hand on, or donate—one
low-self-worth outfit. Make space for the new.

Look at one situation in your life that you feel you should
change but haven't yet. What is the payoff for you in staying
stuck?

If you break your reading deprivation, write about how you did it. In a tantrum? A slip-up? A binge? How do you feel
about it? Why?

Time Travel Exercise 1: List 3 "creative enemies" who
discouraged your creativity

Time Travel Exercise 2: Write out one detailed story
about a creative wound

Write a letter defending yourself against your creative
wounds (mail it to yourself)

Time Travel Exercise 3: List 3 "creative champions"
who encouraged your creativity

Time Travel Exercise 4: Write a thank-you letter to one
of your creative champions

Imaginary Lives: List 5 alternative lives you'd like to
live. Try an activity inspired by one of your imaginary
lives
Take a 20-minute walk with your inner artist

Daily Morning Pages: Write 3 pages longhand every morning (set
alarm 30 mins early)

Read the Basic Principles each morning and night. Be aware of any
skepticism or attitudinal shifts.

New mantra: "Treating myself like a precious object will make me
strong". Paint or write it beautifully and display it where you'll see it daily.

Listen to one side of an album uninterrupted (20 mins). Try doodling
while you listen.

Visit a sacred space (church, library, nature spot) and spend time in
quiet solitude.

Create a comforting atmosphere at home using pleasant scents
(soup, incense, candles, etc.)
Wear your favorite item of clothing for no special occasion.

Buy yourself one wonderful pair of socks, one wonderful pair of
gloves one wonderfully comforting, self-loving something.

Create a collage from magazines (20 min): Collect 10+ magazines,
tear out 20+ images that represent your life/interests, and arrange
on paper in a meaningful way.
List your 5 favorite films and identify common themes

Write down your favorite reading topics and subjects. Are these
topics in your collage?

Created a collage & display your collage somewhere special (can be private) and update
it periodically

1. Creativity is the natural order of life. Life is energy: pure creative energy.
2. There is an underlying, in-dwelling creative forceinfusing all of life -- including ourselves. 
3. When we open ourselves to our creativity, we open
ourselves to the creator's creativity within us and our lives.
4. We are, ourselves, creations. And we, in turn, are meant to continue creativity by being creative ourselves.
5. Creativity is God's gift to us. Using our creativity is our gift back to God.
6. The refusal to be creative is self-will and is counter to our true nature.
7. When we open ourselves to exploring our creativity, we open ourselves to God: good orderly direction.
8. AS we open our creative channel to the creator, many gentle but powerful changes are to be expected.
9. It is safe to open ourselves up to greater and greater creativity."
10. Our creative dreams and yearnings come from a divine source. As we move toward our dreams, we move toward our divinity.

Write down any resistance, angers, and fears you have about going
on from here.

Take a look at your current areas of procrastination. What are the
payoffs in your waiting? Locate the hidden fears. Do a list on paper.

Sneak a peek at Week One, Core Negative Beliefs. Laugh. Yes, the
nasty critters are still there. Note your progress. Read yourself the
affirmations. Write some affirmations about your continued
creativity as you end the course.
Mend any mending.
Repot any pinched and languishing plants.

Select a God jar. It can be a jar, a box, a vase, a container. It’s
something to put your fears, your resentments, your hopes, your
dreams, your worries into.

Use your God jar. Start with your fear list from this week’s fourth
task. When worried, remind yourself it’s in the jar. “God’s got it!”
Then take the next action.

Reflect: Honestly, what would you most like to create?
Open-minded, what oddball paths would you dare to try? Willing,
what appearances are you willing to shed to pursue your dream?

List five people you can talk to about your dreams and with whom
you feel supported to dream and then plan.

Reread the Artist Way book. Share it with a friend. Remember that the miracle
is one artist sharing with another.

Natural Abundance: Collect five interesting rocks as pocket
reminders of creative consciousness

Natural Abundance: Gather and press five flowers or leaves
between wax paper

Clearing: Remove five old clothing items from your closet
(donate or discard)

Creation: Make something in the kitchen (bake or prepare
anything)! Creativity takes many forms.

Communication: Send postcards to five friends you'd love to
hear from

Favorites: Create lists of your favorite: Cars, Vegetables,
Dogs, Desserts, Flowers, Entrées, Trees, Musical Groups,
Fruits, Colors
Environment: Make changes to your living space

Acceptance: Practice saying yes to opportunities that come
your way

Prosperity: Reflect on your financial situation and career
aspirations - add inspiring images to your Image File from
Week 5
